title: Divorce
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Divorce
summary: Scripture takes divorce seriously as a departure from God's design while also extending grace to those who experience it, recognizing that hardness of heart sometimes makes separation unavoidable.
body:
Jesus addressed divorce directly when Pharisees tested him with a question about Mosaic permission. His answer went back to creation: from the beginning, God made them male and female, and what God has joined together, let no one separate [verse:matt.19.6]. Moses permitted divorce, Jesus explained, because of hardness of heart — it was a concession, not an ideal [verse:matt.19.8].

The Old Testament's most direct statement comes through Malachi: God hates divorce, describing it as dealing treacherously with the wife of one's youth [verse:mal.2.16]. The context is covenantal faithfulness — divorce is framed as covenant breaking.

Yet Scripture is not without nuance. Paul addresses mixed marriages where an unbelieving spouse chooses to leave: the believer is not enslaved in such cases [verse:1cor.7.15]. Deuteronomy's regulations on divorce certificates were designed to protect the vulnerable spouse, particularly women who would otherwise be abandoned without legal standing [verse:deut.24.1-4].

God himself uses divorce language metaphorically. Through Jeremiah, God describes giving faithless Israel a certificate of divorce — yet even then extends an invitation to return [verse:jer.3.1,jer.3.8]. The pattern is judgment paired with grace.

Scripture holds the ideal of lifelong covenant together with compassion for broken situations, refusing to collapse into either cheap permissiveness or merciless rigidity.

verses.annotation: Jesus affirms the creation design: what God joined, humans should not separate.
Divorce was permitted due to hard hearts — a concession, not a command.
God's hatred of divorce framed in the context of covenantal treachery.
Paul's exception: if an unbelieving spouse departs, the believer is not bound.
Mosaic divorce regulations designed to protect the vulnerable party.
God uses divorce metaphor for Israel's unfaithfulness — yet still offers restoration.

scholars.quote: Keener notes that Jesus' teaching on divorce was counter-cultural in a world where men could divorce wives for trivial reasons — Jesus elevated marriage by restricting divorce, thereby protecting women.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)

scholars.quote: Fee reads Paul's 'not enslaved' in 1 Corinthians 7:15 as genuine freedom — the abandoned believer is released from the marriage obligation, not left in permanent limbo.
scholars.source: The First Epistle to the Corinthians (Fee, NICNT, 1987)

scholars.quote: Brueggemann observes that God's own use of divorce language in Jeremiah 3 prevents any simplistic reading — if God can speak of divorce while still pursuing reconciliation, the topic resists rigid legalism.
